Indications: Type 2 diabetes mellitus in adults as adjunct to diet and exercise¹
Reduction of major adverse cardiovascular events in adults with T2DM and established cardiovascular disease¹
PACriteria: ICD-10 diagnosis of Type 2 diabetes mellitus (E11.x)
Baseline A1C ≥ 7.0% unless contraindication to other agents
Trial of metformin unless contraindicated²
Inadequate glycemic control after ≥ 3 months of oral therapy²
Documentation of cardiovascular disease if used for CV risk reduction¹

FirstLine: Metformin titrated to maximally tolerated dose²
SecondLine: Addition of GLP-1 receptor agonist or SGLT2 inhibitor based on comorbidity profile²

MedicarePartD: Often requires step therapy with metformin
CommercialPlans: Preferential formulary placement may vary
CV indication may require documentation of ASCVD

2.Title: American Diabetes Association Standards of Care in Diabetes – 2024

Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient has Type 2 diabetes mellitus with inadequate glycemic control despite optimized metformin therapy. Per the American Diabetes Association Standards of Care in Diabetes – 2024, GLP-1 receptor agonists are recommended in patients requiring additional glycemic lowering and cardiovascular risk reduction. Ozempic is FDA-approved for glycemic control and cardiovascular risk reduction in adults with Type 2 diabetes. Delay in escalation increases risk of microvascular and macrovascular complications. Ozempic is medically necessary and consistent with FDA labeling and evidence-based guidelines.

Indications: Chronic weight management in adults with BMI ≥ 30 kg/m² or ≥ 27 kg/m² with at least one weight-related comorbidity¹
PACriteria: Documented BMI criteria
Documentation of ≥ 6 months structured lifestyle modification attempt
Weight-related comorbidity documentation (HTN, dyslipidemia, T2DM)

Lifestyle: Calorie-restricted diet and increased physical activity

ReauthorizationRequirements: ≥ 5% weight reduction from baseline at 6 months

Medicaid: Often excluded from coverage unless state plan allows anti-obesity medications
CommercialPlans: Employer plan dependent
May require documented prior lifestyle attempts

Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient meets FDA criteria for chronic weight management with BMI above threshold and weight-related comorbidity. Sustained obesity increases risk of cardiovascular disease, diabetes, and mortality. Wegovy is FDA-approved for chronic weight management and is medically necessary to reduce long-term cardiometabolic risk.

Indications: Type 2 diabetes mellitus as adjunct to diet and exercise¹
PACriteria: Diagnosis of T2DM
Inadequate control on metformin unless contraindicated

CommercialPlans: Often preferred over other GLP-1s depending on contract

Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: Mounjaro is FDA-approved for Type 2 diabetes management and provides significant A1C reduction. Escalation is consistent with evidence-based diabetes care.

Indications: Chronic weight management in adults meeting BMI criteria¹
PACriteria: BMI ≥ 30 or ≥ 27 with comorbidity
Lifestyle modification attempt documentation

CommercialPlans: Employer-plan dependent coverage

Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: Zepbound is FDA-approved for chronic weight management and is indicated for patients meeting BMI criteria with comorbid risk. Escalation supports reduction in cardiometabolic risk.

Indications: Adjunct to diet and maximally tolerated statin therapy for adults with primary hyperlipidemia including heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia¹
Reduction of risk of myocardial infarction, stroke, and coronary revascularization in adults with established cardiovascular disease¹
Homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia¹
PACriteria: Diagnosis of ASCVD or familial hypercholesterolemia
LDL-C ≥ 70 mg/dL despite maximally tolerated statin therapy²
Trial of ezetimibe unless contraindicated²
Documentation of statin intolerance if applicable

ThirdLine: PCSK9 inhibitor if LDL remains above target despite therapy²

Medicaid: Requires statin failure and ezetimibe trial
May require genetic confirmation for FH
CommercialPlans: Often requires LDL ≥ 100 mg/dL for primary prevention
ASCVD documentation required for CV risk reduction indication

2.Title: 2018 AHA/ACC Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol

Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient has established ASCVD with persistent LDL elevation despite maximally tolerated statin therapy and ezetimibe. Per the 2018 AHA/ACC Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol, PCSK9 inhibitor therapy is recommended for patients who remain above LDL targets despite optimized therapy. Repatha is FDA-approved to reduce cardiovascular events and is medically necessary to mitigate risk of recurrent myocardial infarction or stroke.

Indications: Type 2 diabetes mellitus¹
Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction¹
Chronic kidney disease¹
PACriteria: Diagnosis of T2DM, HFrEF, or CKD
eGFR ≥ labeling threshold¹
Optimized standard therapy for HF or CKD prior to initiation²

HeartFailure: Guideline-directed medical therapy including ACEi/ARB/ARNI and beta-blocker²

Medicaid: May require metformin trial for diabetes indication
HF/CKD indication may bypass diabetes step therapy
CommercialPlans: Typically covered for HF and CKD
May require documentation of ejection fraction

2.Title: 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure

Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient has guideline-defined heart failure and/or chronic kidney disease. Per the 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure, SGLT2 inhibitors are foundational therapy in HFrEF regardless of diabetes status. Farxiga is FDA-approved for these indications and is medically necessary to reduce hospitalization and cardiovascular mortality.

Indications: Nonvalvular atrial fibrillation to reduce stroke risk¹
Treatment and secondary prevention of DVT and PE¹
Postoperative thromboprophylaxis¹
PACriteria: Diagnosis of AFib, DVT, or PE
CHADS-VASc documentation for AFib
No contraindication such as active bleeding

AFib: Direct oral anticoagulant preferred over warfarin unless contraindicated²

Medicaid: May require trial of warfarin in some states
CommercialPlans: Preferred over warfarin for AFib

2.Title: 2019 AHA/ACC/HRS Focused Update on Atrial Fibrillation

Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient has nonvalvular atrial fibrillation with elevated thromboembolic risk. Direct oral anticoagulants are preferred over warfarin in eligible patients per the 2019 AHA/ACC/HRS Focused Update on Atrial Fibrillation. Xarelto is FDA-approved for stroke prevention in AFib and is medically necessary to reduce thromboembolic risk.

Indications: Nonvalvular atrial fibrillation¹
Treatment of DVT and PE¹
Secondary prevention of VTE¹
PACriteria: Documented AFib or VTE
CHADS-VASc score documentation for AFib
No contraindication to anticoagulation

AFib: DOAC preferred over warfarin unless contraindicated²

CommercialPlans: Preferred DOAC in many formularies

2.Title: 2019 AHA/ACC/HRS Focused Update on Atrial Fibrillation

Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient has nonvalvular atrial fibrillation requiring anticoagulation for stroke prevention. Per AHA/ACC/HRS atrial fibrillation guidance, DOAC therapy is preferred over warfarin in eligible patients. Eliquis is FDA-approved and medically necessary to reduce thromboembolic risk.

Indications: Rheumatoid arthritis¹
Psoriatic arthritis¹
Ankylosing spondylitis¹
Crohn’s disease¹
Ulcerative colitis¹
Plaque psoriasis¹
PACriteria: Confirmed autoimmune diagnosis
Moderate-to-severe disease activity
Failure or intolerance of at least one conventional DMARD (e.g., methotrexate)²
Documentation of disease activity scoring when applicable

IBD: Trial of corticosteroids and/or immunomodulator prior to biologic²

CommercialPlans: Prefer biosimilar adalimumab
May require prior biologic failure depending on indication

2.Title: 2021 American College of Rheumatology Guideline for the Treatment of Rheumatoid Arthritis

Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient has moderate-to-severe autoimmune disease with inadequate response to conventional DMARD therapy. Per the American College of Rheumatology guidelines, escalation to biologic therapy is appropriate when disease activity remains uncontrolled. Humira is FDA-approved for this indication and is medically necessary to prevent irreversible joint damage and systemic complications.

Indications: Obstructive sleep apnea confirmed by sleep study¹
PACriteria: AHI ≥ 15 events/hour OR
AHI ≥ 5 with symptoms (daytime sleepiness, hypertension, cardiovascular disease)¹
Sleep study documentation required

Initial: Diagnostic polysomnography or home sleep apnea testing¹

ReauthorizationRequirements: Usage ≥ 4 hours per night on 70% of nights within first 90 days

MedicarePartB: Strict adherence documentation required within 90 days
CommercialPlans: Prior authorization common
May require repeat sleep study if device replacement requested

1.Title: American Academy of Sleep Medicine Clinical Practice Guideline for PAP Therapy

Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient has documented obstructive sleep apnea meeting diagnostic criteria. Untreated OSA increases risk of cardiovascular disease, arrhythmia, stroke, and motor vehicle accidents. CPAP therapy is guideline-recommended first-line treatment and is medically necessary.

Class: Continuous Glucose Monitoring System (DME or Pharmacy Benefit)

Indications: Diabetes mellitus requiring intensive insulin therapy¹
PACriteria: Type 1 or Type 2 diabetes
Multiple daily insulin injections or insulin pump therapy
Frequent glucose monitoring requirement

Initial: Self-monitoring blood glucose with fingerstick prior to CGM

CommercialPlans: Often pharmacy benefit coverage
May require prior authorization

Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient requires intensive insulin therapy with risk of hypoglycemia and glycemic variability. Continuous glucose monitoring improves A1C, reduces hypoglycemia, and supports safer insulin titration. Dexcom G7 is medically necessary to optimize glycemic control and reduce acute complications.

Indications: Schizophrenia¹
Bipolar I disorder¹
Adjunctive treatment of major depressive disorder¹
PACriteria: DSM-5 diagnosis documentation
Failure of first-line therapy when used for MDD augmentation²
Assessment of suicide risk and psychosis severity

MDD: Trial of SSRI or SNRI prior to augmentation²

Medicaid: Preferred atypical antipsychotic in many formularies
Long-acting injectable may require specialty authorization
CommercialPlans: Generic preferred over brand

2.Title: APA Practice Guideline for the Treatment of Patients with Major Depressive Disorder

Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient has a DSM-5 diagnosed psychiatric condition requiring antipsychotic therapy. Aripiprazole is FDA-approved for this indication and is guideline-supported for augmentation in treatment-resistant depression. Continuation is medically necessary to prevent relapse and psychiatric decompensation.

Class: B-lymphocyte stimulator (BLyS) inhibitor (monoclonal antibody; IV or SC)

Indications: Active systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in patients ≥ 5 years receiving standard therapy¹
Active lupus nephritis in patients ≥ 5 years receiving standard therapy¹
PACriteria: ICD-10 diagnosis of systemic lupus erythematosus (M32.x)
Documentation meeting 2019 ACR/EULAR classification criteria
Positive ANA and/or anti-dsDNA antibodies
Persistent moderate disease activity (e.g., SLEDAI ≥ 6) despite standard therapy²³
Active serologic markers (elevated anti-dsDNA and/or low complement levels)²³
Failure or inadequate response to hydroxychloroquine unless contraindicated²
Inability to taper prednisone to ≤ 7.5 mg/day due to flare or disease activity²
Trial of at least one conventional immunosuppressant (MMF, AZA, MTX) with dose and duration documented²³
For lupus nephritis: renal biopsy class III, IV, or V if available⁴
For lupus nephritis: persistent proteinuria ≥ 1 g/g or inadequate partial renal response after 6–12 months of induction therapy⁴

SLEStandardTherapy: Hydroxychloroquine at weight-appropriate dosing (≤ 5 mg/kg/day long-term goal)²
Glucocorticoid therapy with taper toward ≤ 5–7.5 mg/day within 6 months when feasible²
ConventionalImmunosuppressants: Cyclophosphamide for organ- or life-threatening disease²
LupusNephritisProtocol: Glucocorticoids plus mycophenolic acid analog (2–3 g/day)⁴
Consider triple therapy (GC + MPAA + belimumab or CNI) if inadequate response⁴
Escalation recommended if failure to achieve partial renal response by 6–12 months⁴

ReauthorizationRequirements: ≥ 4-point reduction in SLEDAI score consistent with SRI-4 benchmark¹
For lupus nephritis: reduction in proteinuria and stabilization or improvement in eGFR⁴

Medicaid: Requires documentation of hydroxychloroquine and immunosuppressant failure
Lupus nephritis may require biopsy class documentation
MedicarePartD: Reauthorization tied to objective SLEDAI improvement
CommercialPlans: Often require ≥ 6 months of inadequate response to conventional therapy
Autoantibody-positive SLE frequently required
Separate approval pathways for SLE vs lupus nephritis indication
OperationalConsiderations: IV formulation may require infusion center authorization
SC formulation may process under pharmacy benefit

2.Title: 2025 American College of Rheumatology Guideline for the Treatment of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus

3.Title: 2019 Update of the EULAR Recommendations for the Management of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus

4.Title: 2024 American College of Rheumatology Guideline for the Screening, Treatment, and Management of Lupus Nephritis

Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient has confirmed systemic lupus erythematosus with persistent moderate disease activity, active serologic markers, and steroid dependence despite optimized hydroxychloroquine and conventional immunosuppressant therapy. Per the 2025 American College of Rheumatology Guideline for the Treatment of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus and the 2019 EULAR Recommendations, escalation to biologic therapy is appropriate when disease activity remains uncontrolled and glucocorticoid tapering is not achievable. If lupus nephritis is present, the 2024 American College of Rheumatology Lupus Nephritis Guideline supports incorporation of belimumab into combination therapy when partial renal response is not achieved within 6–12 months. Delayed biologic escalation increases risk of flare, cumulative organ damage, prolonged glucocorticoid toxicity, and hospitalization. Benlysta is FDA-approved and medically necessary.
